summary: I was born in Ban Vinai Refugee Camp in 1992, a place in Thailand that housed many Hmong people like my family after the Vietnam War. Ban Vinai was more than just a camp; it was a refuge for those displaced by conflict, including my parents who lived there before we immigrated. Growing up, I felt connected to Ban Vinai through the stories and the bracelet my father made before we left. It symbolizes my heritage and the sacrifices my family made to build a new life.

Thailand is where I was born, specifically in the Ban Vinai Refugee Camp, in 1992. My family and many other Hmong refugees lived there after the Vietnam War, seeking safety and a chance to rebuild. Thailand was a temporary home that shaped my early life before we immigrated to the United States in 1993. Even though I left when I was very young, Thailand remains an important part of my identity and family history.

The Vietnam War had a profound impact on my family and our history. After the war, many Hmong people, including my parents, were displaced and ended up in refugee camps like Ban Vinai in Thailand. This conflict forced them to leave their homes and eventually led to our immigration to the United States in 1993. The war's aftermath is a big part of why my family values education and perseverance so deeply.

My family immigrated to the United States in 1993 when I was just a year old, leaving behind the refugee camp in Thailand. The US became the place where I grew up, went to school, and where my parents encouraged me to push myself academically. It’s here that I’ve been able to pursue opportunities that my mother never had, and the bracelet my father made before we came serves as a reminder of our journey and the sacrifices made to reach this new life.
